speaker: interviewer
text: It's June 21, 1995. And we're interviewing survivor, Abraham Melezin. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: To make it short--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, are we ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: I am, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: To make it short, we opened the door. I jumped. On the last wagon was machine gun. They shoot. Right-- I went. And I started to go back to the territory I know is very anti-Semitic. So I was going to be very careful. But to my-- to my surprise I was greeted as is-- all the time [NON-ENGLISH]-- means 'senior'-- 'Mr. Senior.' You know, the posture and the boots of whatever or not-- so I look like one. And I went, you know, through all this part of Lublin district-- crossed, forded the river, one river. I forded the other river. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Got help from peasants, who always took me for a, a, a man from the-- from the forest. I had the general passport, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was, more or less, valid in all Poland. I come from far away and go far away-- is what to [? say-- ?] I would greet. Otherwise, I would greet you. I would come into your house. I would cross myself. I greet, you know, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the [? lauded ?] in our territories. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I got caught in a place called [NON-ENGLISH]. Simply-- I was going-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried, you know, to, to make a shortcut and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] encountered two German gendarmes, I don't know. And they took me there. And that-- their officer, drunkard, some kind of-- what, what he did-- whatever he did, he said, shoot him. Shoot him. Well, they gave me a-- they gave me a shovel, took me out behind the town, [NON-ENGLISH]. I dug out. And all the time, they drank. And they were completely drunk. Finally, one went to, at least-- you know, to let water out. And the other finally said, OK, that's enough, he says. He unbuckled his revolver and gave me the shovel. And that moment I saw-- [LAUGHS] so I dug myself. [LAUGHS] He says, everything now-- I dug about an hour, maybe more. But then, everything what happened to us probably takes longer to tell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fight. And he say, give me the shovel. I gave him the shovel. And this way, I took the shovel, this way. So he put his hand, you know, his right hand, you know, his right hand to shield himself and-- with the revolver. And I hit him right here. And I saw how his part of hand, you know, hangs only-- It's a-- And that-- and he started, you know-- he just cries, whatever it is. And I hit the revolver-- I, I grabbed his revolver. And I pulled to the other one, who started to look at me very surprised, and didn't shoot, you know. And then I said, you know, you idiot, you know. You have to cock, you know, first of all. So I cocked. And I went-- [POINTS]. I didn't kill neither one. But I hit the other one twice. And I ran. There were, you know, fish ponds. I slipped into a fish pond. And the rain started. And I run into forest, [NON-ENGLISH] Forest, got lost. I circled it for several days, you know. Once, once-- I was deadly tired. I was deadly-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was wet. All the time it was raining. It was-- and finally, I came up to a place near, near Mosty. Because I decided I'm going back to Zapole. I have their armaments. And I'm going to make a order with a couple of people. And then I'll go to-- I know where to go to hit Russian partisans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I, I had a very good-- I-- they would take me because I have very good armaments. I had two revolvers, an automatic, and about five grenades, you know. So they would take me this way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mosty-- and there was a small [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] watering hole. I had to cross it. But what I saw is somebody threw in inside the pieces of implements. And I couldn't cross it. Everything was stuck, stuck out, you know. Well, I was caught in Mosty. I was brought to Volkovysk. I sat in Volkovysk until it was-- so all of '43, you know, '43, until approximately [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 24th, 24th of December. I was-- I went through Gestapo in Volkovysk. I put the Gestapo, showed how stupid they are. And they decided, at the time, really the one who I'm telling that I-- my mother is from Georgia, and so on. It's a long story-- separate. They send me to Bialystok. I had another interrogation with Gestapo. And they sent me to Stutthof, with 'SB,' [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Special handling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Special treatment.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does that mean when they said, 'special treatment?'

speaker: interviewee
text: [POINTING] And on the third day, I was called out. Later, I found out that this was the gas chamber. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they put me the room. They told me go to the next door. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On top was a, you know, a dangling electrical bulb. And right under the bulb, I look, you know, there is blood. So I turned around. And there was a Hauptsturmfuhrer Meyer with a revolver. He went, turn around.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, shoot me the way I am. What is the matter with you? Are you afraid? I am not afraid. Turn around you, [SPEAKING GERMAN] whatever or not, and so on. All the time, I was a Pole, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He, he came up to me, you know, and hit me with the butt of the revolver, knocking out upper teeth, you know. So I felt everything gets warm. So I spit out. And I said to him, oh, you can tell-- it is-- it is a very clean work-- job, I said. You can tell Colonel Haushoffer that you knocked out teeth from Mielzynski's mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, Haushoffer? Haushoffer? I go, yeah. And I felt that, you know, I'm getting a little bit such-- you know. And Haushoffer was the famous German professor who made the geopolitik, you know, the theoretician.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I just spoke out, without thinking specially of something. And then he started to hit me with the revolver, with his legs, and whatever or not. Well, when I woke up-- so I, I dreamed that I am swimming. By the way, I don't know how to swim. You know, so I was somewhere in the corner. And a German put-- threw on me water-- get up! Get up! Get up! I returned. My number was 32,209. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went through Stutthof. And I got to a very big block-- barrack. And there, I felt that, you know, there was one-- a former petty officer from Polish Navy. And the moment he knew that we're intelligentsia-- so he tried to do everything what he could to maltreat us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [NON-ENGLISH]-- it is commander, you know-- everybody who, who had to cut wood, and then to carry them. What they used to do, somebody cut wood, a big timber, you know, so they can put a big man like I, and put another on small one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pick it up. How I picked it up, I don't know. Because later, I tried to pick up a much smaller thing with my brother, who is also-- was quite a guy. So we couldn't even move. But, you know, at such a moment-- anyway from that [NON-ENGLISH] usually after a couple days, people would disappear. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But one day-- it was the third day I was standing with [NON-ENGLISH]. Somebody comes up. It was [NON-ENGLISH] it seems to me from Arbeiteinsantz. It means 'labor bureau.' And he called out-- there is no names over there, you know. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That's 509. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. After I-- he went through. So I stuttered. It will be the end. He looked at me. And [GERMAN]-- turn to the right! 50 times around the block! So I ran around the block. And he-- and he stood there. And the commander stood there. Schneller, schneller! And he started, you know, as if he made-- as if he hit, but he didn't hit. Schneller! But schneller-- I got around schneller. I made three times around. I saw the commander went. And then he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Polish-- stop. Or go to the barracks, he said. On the upper side there on my barrack, and sleep over until the commander will return. And then register with the commander and go back to your block. We know who you are. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] You know, so I thank him. Don't. And then the next day, I was transferred to a different commander. No, the next day I was took to a place where I could rest. It was are some kind of a-- I don't know what they did with old clothes, or whatever it is. And then I was sent to a different commander. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Still, it was very bad because I was in a very bad block. One day, the Blockfuhrer, block leader, told me to clean the-- to work the floor. And I was sitting as such, you know, and rubbing, whatever or not. Through the block came in two-- as we call them, [NON-ENGLISH], you know. That was 'permanent people.' They talk something to that-- to the block. Then one came up to me, picked me up, you know, with one hand, stood me up and said, get out from here. This is the block not for you. And they put me-- and the two put me on the block number five, one of the best in the concentration camp. On one side was a group of Poles with the number 17,000 something. It was a group of young people who [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were denounced in Gdynia and arrested. On the other side were Danes and Norwegians.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with non-Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All the time, I was, you know, with red triangle, with letter P, Pole. And there, one young Pole by the name Felix Lada started to take care of me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Little by little, he organized for me different clothes, different everything. I got befriended with a Dane who worked in Scheinderei. It means in 'tailor shop.' He made for me at that shop. The other one worked in Tischlerei. It means in some building. He made for me a pipe from cherry wood. I had a pipe, too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I worked outside. And the buildings commander would build some kind of factory in a firm called [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It's German-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's, it's a military-- some kind of military enterprise. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then came-- the kapo was changed. It was a Russian-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a Russian soldier and very bad. He hit me with a two by four. And here I broke my some-- me vertebra-- whatever is the word there. Later, I was-- I-- but little by little, you know-- I-- when I came for the first time into the concentration camp, of [NON-ENGLISH], you know. So the first day, I looked over the window, where I saw one single picture, a wagon full of dead quote, unquote 'people' who were people, and 10, 10 [NON-ENGLISH]. It means 10 inmates dragging the wagon. And one kapo in back with a whip, you know. They did everything to dehumanize myself, you know. There was no end to beating, no matter of anything, no matter what-- the, the very first two weeks, with my taking cart to this, you know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then, the very same [NON-ENGLISH] came up. I said to him when I was working in the field, we-- quasi-inspection. So he said to me, Meilzynski, the transport list-- the first page from your transport list got lost. They cannot reconstruct it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And three weeks or so later, the whole transfer from Bialystock who came was gassed. So I and a lawyer, Duszinski from Poland, survived. Because I was on the first page-- and Duszinski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Melezin, how long were you at Stutthof? When did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came-- --finally

speaker: interviewer
text: Leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Stutthof-- I came to Stutthof, I think, about January 10th or maybe 15th of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you leave? Under what circumstances did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: In January-- on January 25, 1945, we were evacuated. It was a death march. Where, where took-- they took us. It, it was a very strong winter of snow, and cold, and so on. And we marched. And we marched in big columns, zigzagging, a sign there said west. Because when they took us over, on the river side, we knew that we were going west. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after a few-- you know, it's an interesting moment. A few moments after we left the-- we left-- we left, we came to a railroad. And there was a narrow-gauge train running, full of Germans with packages, with their [INAUDIBLE], all their [NON-ENGLISH], whatever or not. And I said, well, if I live long enough to see this, I can die.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did people--

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: --die on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --10 Days--

speaker: interviewer
text: --death--

speaker: interviewee
text: --10 days we zigzagged, zigzagged, zigzagged. And finally, I wound up in the place. And there was typhoid fever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people, whoever would fall or want to just, you know, to just-- to have some [INAUDIBLE] would be killed right away. After 10 days, we came to the village Nawitz. I was-- and two blocks there, fifth and seven. And, and Danes-- I said Danes, Norwegians, and Poles from block seven and Poles from block five.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody-- three quarters were sick with typhoid fever. So the-- whoever-- the leader, the German SS, called out the doctor. But that man, the doctor, didn't want to go. He said, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, look. When I was a child, I had typhoid fever. I have immunity for life. I'll organize the hospital. I organized the hospital. They put us in some kind of a shack.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- the very first thing what I did, I requisitioned several cots and cut them to close the holes in the wall, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were there since-- we marched about 10 days, you know. Later, the distance between the Nawitz-- the, the village Nawitz-- and Stutthof is about 85-90 kilometers. But in 10 days, we probably made 250 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you liberated from this hospital while you were at the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then on the March-- on the March 6th or 7th, a commission came. And they said that they will take away the hospital to Lemberg, but-- to Lemberg. But we know that they were going to shoot us. Because in the evening, when the commission, the commission or the SS men went away-- so the [NON-ENGLISH], the guard, came up to me, and said, look. They are going to shoot you on the 13th of September. But you, who did so many for these people, he said, oh-- he said, oh, I can save you. You know, you go out every day. And I learn the-- I teach-- I teach the dog to recognize you. So I used to go out. And he used to teach the dog, Albert. And I got friendly with the dog. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the night from March 9th to 10th, he ran into me about 2 o'clock. Yeah, I had to go out. But he ran into the hospital-- no, in the barrack about-- and said, we are being evacuated. And he took out a golden watch with a golden chain. Take it, you know. You're very good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, look, Hans, I said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how you got it. Keep it. But I remember you. And thank you for teaching me-- and to befriend it with Albert.

speaker: interviewee
text: He turned around. In the moment, he came in with his backpack, pulled out a camera. He told me-- he goes-- I can swear I, I bought it for my money. Take it. Use it. And they left. In the morning, March 10th, I came out. It was gray morning. There was shooting around. On the porch where the SS men lived was one single man. It was Schutzpolizei, however, not SS man-- bicycle, a rifle, and he had binoculars. And he looked, looked, looked. I-- to the right, I heard, you know, shooting. I looked around. But there was a haze still, you know, early morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started to go toward the porch. So he grabbed the rifle, stop! I said, I'm a doctor. [NON-ENGLISH], I said. I came up to the porch-- a boy 15, maybe 16. He was so afraid. He said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Give me the binoculars. I take a look. And here, in front, there is a highway. I saw German [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] cars running in one direction towards Lemberg, on the other side, hills. And from the hills-- in front of the hills, you know, about maybe-- like, I could-- maybe 100 meters from the highway, three tanks. And behind them, the whole line of Russian soldiers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to him-- to that boy, I said, give me the binoculars. Take your rifle. Take the bicycle. Go in the first house. Change clothes. Burn it. Take civil, civil clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he, he picked up-- the Germans, you know, the order-- he picked up the rifle. Well, he said, I cannot give you these binoculars, he says, government property. He said, go. I could kill him there, or not, a boy, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he left. I went to the block five, to my boys. I said, sit quiet. Sit quiet. A couple hours, you won't get any-- you won't get breakfast. But you, you will get Red Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's how you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came about 12:00 noon [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a Chevy truck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We liberated you, whatever or not, you know. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll move the story along if we can. Perhaps you could just tell us briefly after the liberation--

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --where you went before coming to the United States, Dr. Melezin.

speaker: interviewee
text: I rode with-- I got my own horse to Bialystock. There, I disposed of the horse. I found out what happened to the Jews. Because I went on purpose to the-- to town, to shtetl [NON-ENGLISH]. I saw that-- they told me that all kikes-- they kicked all kikes out of such a-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out, at that point, what happened to your wife and your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I, I didn't find anything. I didn't find anything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I changed the horse for salami and kielbasa, went to Bialystock, registered as a Jew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Went to Warsaw, trying to find somebody from the university who might know. But I found one former assistant [NON-ENGLISH]. But it was that we have full, full enrollment, so to speak, all of the staff. But he suggested I should go to Lublin, where there was a new university. I went to Lublin. I went there. I introduced myself with my proper name, yes. They knew-- they heard of me. I didn't know anybody. But one I know, one. A lady I knew, a Professor [NON-ENGLISH]. Right away, I was taken as an assistant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you remain at Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remained there until the end of 1947. In-between, I was naive. I was-- I went all through all the concentration camps, looking for my wife and my child. I was naive. I thought maybe I'd find something. And I, I remember what she wore. She made a big mistake. She had a safe house in Rulewicze. And instead, she decided to join the family. These were the-- this is what the family bonds can do. She would-- she would survive. I wouldn't. I could perish. But she surely would survive with the child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: [MOAN] You know, Rachela has a passport what is set one way. I got a passport one way and back. It is because the, the minister, foreign minister, was a geographer. And when I had to extend my passport, so I get a regular passport. I came on a ship, in the first class. The American Consul, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by the name, [? Lelich, ?] was a very good man. He told me what to do, what to avoid. I told that I am going to Paris. But I bought a ticket to United States. He told me to buy the first-class ticket. And I came on January 17th, year--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to New York on the ship And I was outraged. Here that visa, getting the visa and the passport was a problem. Because I had to bribe and do whatever or not. Here, he takes my passport, pulls out the visa, takes a look-- takes a look. You were a professor at Lublin University, he says. And the interpreter, a lady, repeats it, you know. I agree, yes. And he throws it in-- this away.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to stop.
